title: Dyeing with marigold petals
subtitle: A reliable yellow-gold dye from fresh or dried Calendula and Tagetes flowers using an alum mordant
excerpt: Marigold petals give a clear, warm yellow to gold on wool and other protein fibres. Both Calendula officinalis and Tagetes erecta work well as dye plants. The colour is relatively lightfast on alum-mordanted wool and does not require post-mordanting. Fresh or dried petals give similar results.
sourceNotes: Marigold as a dye plant is documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999) and Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes (2007). The alum mordant method described is standard UK natural dyeing practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Potassium aluminium sulphate (alum) dissolved in water and used to treat protein fibres before dyeing. Alum attaches to the fibre and provides bonding sites for dye molecules, improving colour uptake and lightfastness. It is the mildest and most commonly used mordant in natural dyeing and is considered safe to handle with basic precautions.

A dye bath from which most of the available dye pigment has already been taken up by the fibre. Successive skeins dyed in the same bath produce progressively lighter shades as the bath exhausts. An exhaust bath is useful for producing a tonal range from a single dye plant.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Alum is low hazard but wear gloves and avoid inhaling the powder. Do not use dye pots for food.

content.text: Marigold is one of the most reliable natural dyes for beginners. The colour is strong and the process is forgiving. The
needed to prepare the fibre is easy to source from home-brewing or cheesemaking suppliers. The yellow ranges from lemon-gold on pale wool to deep amber on pre-mordanted dark fleece. The
gives lighter yellows on each successive skein.

items.name: Wool yarn or fleece, scoured and wet out
Fresh or dried marigold petals (Calendula or Tagetes)
Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 5 litre

content.text: Dissolve the alum in a small amount of hot water in the dye pot. Add enough warm water to allow the fibre to move freely. Add the wet wool. Heat slowly to 80°C over 30-40 minutes. Hold at 80°C for 45 minutes without boiling. Remove the wool, squeeze gently, and set aside. The mordanted wool can be dyed immediately or stored damp for a few days.

Place the petals in the clean dye pot with enough water to cover generously. Heat to 80°C and simmer for 45 minutes. Strain out all plant material. The liquid should be a deep amber-yellow. Return the dye liquid to the pot and top up with water if needed to cover the wool.

Add the mordanted, damp wool to the dye bath at the same temperature as the bath (to avoid thermal shock). Heat slowly to 80°C and hold for 45-60 minutes, turning the wool occasionally. Remove the wool, rinse in water of the same temperature, then rinse again in cooler water. Squeeze gently and dry away from direct sun.

items.problem: The yellow colour is very pale even with a full amount of petals
items.cause: Petals were added to the bath without sufficient extraction time
items.fix: Simmer the petals for at least 45 minutes before straining. Dried petals need longer extraction than fresh; add an extra 15 minutes.

items.problem: The colour fades significantly after the first wash
items.cause: Fibre was not properly mordanted, or alum quantity was too low
items.fix: Use 15 g of alum per 100 g of dry fibre weight. Under-mordanted wool will not hold marigold dye reliably.
